sub: Votes, dares, showdowns — the group performs and decides.

1.name: Warm the Room
q: Snap vote: coffee people to one side of the room, tea people to the other, right now — no in-between allowed.
Everyone predicts, out loud on three, what time tonight actually wraps up. Closest guess gets bragging rights later.
On three, everyone holds up the number of snackable things within arm's reach. Highest number has to explain themselves.
Vote: who in this room negotiates hardest for a better seat, table, or parking spot. They don't get a vote in their own nomination.
Quickfire: name this group's unofficial theme song in one title. First answer that gets three nods sticks for the night.
Point at whoever replies to texts the fastest. Reveal on three — the winner has ten seconds to prove it.
Rate out loud, on three, how likely tonight is to become a "remember when" story. Biggest number and smallest number have to swap reasons.
Silent vote: point at who's most likely already plotting a graceful early exit. Reveal on three, no hard feelings.
Fastest hand up: name everyone currently in this room, correctly, in under ten seconds.
Split the room: one side for "planned tonight in advance," the other for "showed up and winged it." No standing in the middle.
Blind guess: without looking around, call out how many people here are wearing something with a pocket. Closest guess wins.
Point to the two people here most likely to start a business together by accident. Reveal on three — they get thirty seconds to name what it sells.
Quick poll: has tonight been a loud room or a quiet room so far. Majority rules, minority gets ten seconds to protest.
On three, everyone names the last app they opened before this one. Funniest overlap between two answers wins.
Show of hands: who's already messaged someone about how tonight's going. No details needed, just the hand.
Silent vote: point at who's most likely to still be telling tonight's story a year from now. Reveal on three.
Predict it: does the next round of this game get louder or quieter than this one. Vote now, get held to it later.
Hold up fingers for how many new things you've learned about this room tonight. Highest number names one, right now.
Vote: who here would make the calmest emergency phone call if something actually went wrong. No self-nominations counted.
Quickfire ranking: sort the room from fastest talker to slowest, out loud, by group agreement, in under thirty seconds.
Show of hands: who's already changed their mind about something small tonight. No need to say what it was.
Vote: who's most likely to have a playlist they'd never admit to out loud. Confirm or deny — no proof required either way.
On three, guess out loud how many people here have already misplaced their phone tonight, however briefly. Closest guess wins.
Vote: who's the room's designated adult when things go sideways. Reveal on three — they have to accept the badge.
Call it now: name one topic that's guaranteed to come up again before tonight's over. Get proven right or wrong later.
Quick poll: is this group more "same page" or "wildly different opinions" tonight. Vote, then name the biggest outlier.

2.name: Challenges
q: Do a slow-motion action replay of the last thing that actually happened in this room, narrated like sports commentary.
Say the Wi-Fi password — real or invented — like it's the most romantic thing you've ever said.
Answer the next question anyone asks you only as a movie villain. Group decides when the bit's over.
Give a weather forecast for your own mood right now. Fronts, pressure systems, all of it.
Explain what you're wearing tonight like a red-carpet interview, using only what's actually on you.
Freestyle ten seconds about this exact room. Rhyming optional, full commitment mandatory.
Narrate your own entrance into this room, in third person, like a nature documentary that's already rolling.
Do an impression of this group trying to whisper. Everyone else rates your volume control.
Act out one side of a dramatic phone call. The room guesses who's on the other end, and why they're upset.
Hold a full argument with the nearest object like it just insulted you.
Walk across the room like you just won something you definitely didn't earn.
Give a five-second infomercial pitch for the most useless thing you can reach right now.
Recreate your worst moment this week as a 15-second silent film. No sound, huge gestures.
Answer three questions in an accent the group picks for you. No breaking it early.
Reveal anything in your pocket or bag like it's the star prize on a game show.
Read out the most boring parts of your day today like breaking news.
Give your expert opinion, expert voice and all, on a topic you know nothing about, chosen by the group.
Do your best impression of the last stranger who mildly annoyed you. Group guesses the scenario.
Deliver a break-up speech to a food you genuinely love.
Give this exact room a half-time pep talk like the stakes are enormous.
Show us the entrance you'd want at your own surprise party. Full walk, full energy, right now.
React to winning an award for the most oddly specific achievement the group can invent in five seconds.
Give directions to the nearest exit like you're narrating the final act of an action movie.
Hum a song the group names, badly on purpose, until someone guesses it correctly.
Deliver your own voicemail greeting like you're famous now and mildly inconvenienced by it.
Give the most dramatic reading possible of whatever nearby object has words on it — receipt, label, anything.

3.name: Showdown
q: Vote: who here is most likely to talk their way out of an actual arrest. Winner gets 15 seconds to defend it or nominate someone worse — the room's re-vote is final.
Smash together "a vending machine" and "a therapist." 60 seconds to pitch the room on why it's the future. Loudest reaction wins the round.

Phone privately gives one player a secret word to hide. Room picks an everyday topic out loud, then talks about it for 60 seconds while the briefed player quietly works their secret word into the conversation. Room votes on who was hiding something.

Phone gives two equally ridiculous options. The room splits to opposite sides in five seconds flat, no fence-sitting. Twenty seconds for each side to out-argue the other into switching — whichever side's smaller at the end owes the room a forfeit.
The group votes, right now, on this session's most dramatic performance so far. Winner gives a 15-second acceptance speech — thank someone, and cry if you can manage it.
Someone calls a dare. Anyone can accept it, or raise it into something bigger for the next person. Keep raising until someone commits fully or the room agrees it's peaked.
Full-room thumb war tournament, straight knockout, ten-second matches. Every loser has to narrate their own defeat like a ring announcer before they sit down.
The group is deciding a new rule for the rest of tonight, right now. Proposals get ten seconds, arguments get thirty, then a vote — whatever passes is binding until the next showdown card.
Phone names something ridiculous already in the room. Everyone bids fake enthusiasm out loud — loudest, most dramatic "bid" wins it, and owes the room a ten-second speech on why it was worth it.
Phone gives the group the last line of a story. Going around, everyone adds the sentence that comes before it, working backwards, until someone declares the true beginning.
Phone calls a scene — "the moment the aliens landed at a barbecue." Ten seconds to freeze into it as a group. Best freeze gets the photo taken, worst freeze gets a re-shoot.
Phone privately assigns two players opposite, ridiculous positions on the same topic. Thirty seconds each, no interrupting, then the room votes the winner by applause.
Everyone secretly ranks the room from "first to bail on a plan" to "last to bail." Reveal all at once on three — the biggest disagreement between two rankings gets settled on the spot.

One player secretly counts to a number only they know. Pass the phone hand to hand while the group hums any tune together — whoever's holding it when they call time owes the room one dramatic sentence, on the spot.
Vote: who's most likely to become the group's accidental leader in an actual crisis. They get 15 seconds to accept the role or nominate a replacement — the room's final vote decides it.
Someone quietly takes a nearby object hostage — nothing valuable. The room has 60 seconds to negotiate, bribe, or perform its way into getting it back.
Everyone tosses one small item from their pocket or bag into the middle, then grabs a different one at random. Ten seconds each to explain why that item obviously belongs to them now.
Phone privately tells two players to swap one opinion or habit for the next 60 seconds — they argue as each other when the room fires questions. Room decides who nailed the impression.

Phone names a silly trait — "best in a crisis," "most likely to make a friend anywhere." Two captains draft the whole room onto teams for it, one at a time, out loud. Whoever's picked last gives the victory speech anyway.
Straight knockout rhyme battle: phone gives a word, two players trade rhymes until one hesitates or repeats. Loser's out, winner faces the next challenger.

Phone fires rapid-fire questions about the room itself — who's been awake longest, who's travelled furthest to be here tonight, who ate most recently. Fastest correct-enough answer scores a point; first to three wins bragging rights.

The room is put on trial for a fictional group crime the phone invents. One player defends everyone in thirty seconds flat, then the room delivers a verdict — guilty means a group forfeit, not guilty means bragging rights.
Everyone secretly predicts who'll come out on top of the next showdown card, whatever it ends up being. Reveal it now — anyone who guessed right gets to skip their next dare.
Two players get the same line from the phone in two different styles — one dramatic, one deadpan. Both perform back to back; the room decides the winner by volume of applause alone.
One player chooses, on behalf of the whole room, between two ridiculous options someone else names on the spot. Everyone else lives with it — no complaints, no re-votes.

Everyone quietly thinks of the most chaotic thing that's happened in this exact room tonight. Whoever's willing raises a hand on three — the room votes which raised hand is hiding the best story, and they have to tell it.

wild: Narrate the next 20 seconds of real room activity using only sound effects — no words allowed.
Someone hums a wake-up jingle; the whole group has to "wake up" from a fake nap on cue. Judge the most dramatic waker.
Phone whispers a ridiculous phrase to one player. Pass it around the whole circle by whisper, one ear at a time — last person says it out loud, compare it to the original.
Twenty seconds: everyone switches to the accent the group just voted on, mid-conversation, no warning when it's over.
Phone calls a pose category — "most dramatic," "most confused," "most caught-off-guard." Everyone strikes it solo, at the same time, on three.
Pass any nearby object around the circle while the group hums a tune together. Whoever's holding it when the humming stops gives it a ten-second toast.
Thirty seconds, total silence: everyone mirrors the movements of the person across from them. See who breaks first.
Phone names a category. Everyone races to touch something in the room that fits it — last to touch owes the room a forfeit.
Whoever's holding the phone narrates the next full minute of real group conversation like a nature documentary. Everyone else acts along with whatever gets narrated.
On three, the whole group becomes one orchestra using only voices — everyone "plays" an instrument sound for ten seconds. No rehearsal allowed.
Pick one: recite the alphabet backwards, your own phone number, or tonight's plan — in a single breath. Group judges whether it counted.
Strike a pose for "how I felt this morning," then immediately one for "how I feel right now." Let the group guess the gap.
Phone hands one player a wildly specific fake prediction about their coming week. Deliver it like a genuine psychic — straight face, full commitment.
Go around the circle building one sentence about this group, one word at a time, clapping the rhythm as it grows. First to break the clap restarts it.
Everyone claims the nearest object as their weapon for an imaginary duel. Phone calls two names — they narrate the whole showdown in slow motion, group declares the winner.
Phone gives the room a single word. Everyone tries to work it into normal conversation within the next two minutes without announcing it — spot who's struggling.
final: Last card. Everyone up — whatever's in reach goes in the air: glass, cup, phone, fist, doesn't matter.
One big vote, no lobbying: point at the best moment of the whole night. On three, the room decides.
Whoever wins it stays standing one extra second. Cheers to them — and to this exact group, right here, right now.
